data.reasoningText:
The user wants me to symbolicate the .NET runtime frames in the backtrace. The key library is `libcoreclr.so` with Build ID `4ca7e2f19b3d605847a1c8e3f5b7d9a2c4e6f801`.

To symbolicate these frames, I would need the debug symbols for this specific build of `libcoreclr.so`. The Build ID `4ca7e2f19b3d605847a1c8e3f5b7d9a2c4e6f801` identifies the exact binary.

.NET runtime symbols are published by Microsoft. I can try to download the symbols from the Microsoft symbol server. The .NET runtime debug symbols are typically available at `https://msdl.microsoft.com/download/symbols/` or via `dotnet symbol`.


I'll attempt to fetch the symbols from Microsoft's symbol server using the Build ID. The URL format for ELF binaries follows a specific pattern with the buildid embedded in the path, or I could try the debuginfod protocol instead. I'll try using debuginfod to fetch the symbols from Microsoft's server, then use addr2line or llvm-symbolizer to convert those memory offsets into readable function names and line numbers.

arguments.command:
# Let's try to download a recent .NET 10 android-arm64 runtime package and extract libcoreclr.so
# to check its build ID and verify the symbol server format
cd /tmp
VERSION="10.0.7"
NUPKG_URL="https://api.nuget.org/v3-flatcontainer/microsoft.netcore.app.runtime.android-arm64/${VERSION}/microsoft.netcore.app.runtime.android-arm64.${VERSION}.nupkg"
echo "Downloading Microsoft.NETCore.App.Runtime.android-arm64 ${VERSION}..."
curl -sL -o runtime-android-arm64.nupkg "$NUPKG_URL" 2>&1
ls -lh runtime-android-arm64.nupkg
echo "---"
# Extract just libcoreclr.so
mkdir -p runtime-extract
cd runtime-extract
unzip -o ../runtime-android-arm64.nupkg "runtimes/android-arm64/native/libcoreclr.so" 2>&1
echo "---"
# Check the build ID
file runtimes/android-arm64/native/libcoreclr.so
echo "---"
readelf -n runtimes/android-arm64/native/libcoreclr.so 2>&1 | grep -A2 "Build ID"
